rateLimitingStep: Hydrolysis of the covalent glycosyl-enzyme intermediate

steps.title: Substrate binding and distortion
steps.description: The peptidoglycan hexasaccharide binds along the A–F subsite cleft. The sugar in subsite D is forced into a strained half-chair / 1,4-boat conformation, pre-organising it for reaction by destabilising the ground state (Koshland's 'substrate distortion').
steps.notes: Trp62, Trp63 and Trp108 line the substrate cleft and provide stacking contacts with the sugar rings.

steps.title: Glu35 protonates the glycosidic oxygen
steps.description: Glu35, sitting in a hydrophobic microenvironment (elevated pKa ~6.5), donates its proton to the glycosidic oxygen between subsites D and E. This primes departure of the sugar E product.

steps.title: Asp52 attacks C1 — covalent glycosyl-enzyme forms
steps.description: Asp52 carboxylate attacks the C1 anomeric carbon of the sugar at subsite D through a transition state with strong oxocarbenium character. The leaving group (sugar E onward) departs. A covalent β-glycosyl-enzyme intermediate is formed (Vocadlo & Davies, 2001 — revised Koshland mechanism).

steps.title: Glu35 activates water
steps.description: Glu35, now deprotonated, acts as the general base and abstracts a proton from an incoming water molecule positioned in the vacated subsite E.

steps.title: Water hydrolyses the glycosyl-enzyme
steps.description: The activated hydroxide attacks C1 of the covalent intermediate through a second oxocarbenium-like transition state. The C1-Asp52 bond breaks, releasing the sugar D product with net retention of configuration.

references.citation: Vocadlo DJ, Davies GJ, Laine R, Withers SG. Catalysis by hen egg-white lysozyme proceeds via a covalent intermediate. Nature 412:835–838 (2001).
Phillips DC. The hen egg-white lysozyme molecule. Proc Natl Acad Sci USA 57:483–495 (1967).
Koshland DE. Stereochemistry and the mechanism of enzymatic reactions. Biol Rev 28:416–436 (1953).
